topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The knock on the door sounded like an accusation. I stared at the delivery notification, a small, brown box sitting accusingly on the welcome mat. It was addressed to the former resident, a name I vaguely recalled from the lease agreement. My jaw tightened, the muscle jumping beneath my skin. I snatched the package, the cardboard rough beneath my fingers, and marched straight to the mailroom. When I saw the label, a vein pulsed visibly in my temple. "Seriously?" I muttered, my voice dangerously low. I ripped open the tape with unnecessary force, the sound echoing in the quiet hallway. Inside, nestled amongst packing peanuts, was a stack of old, stained linens. I crumpled the paper in my fist, the frustration bubbling up into a hot, tight pressure in my chest. This was an absolute waste of my time.

[story 2]

I found the package tucked beside the recycling bin, a glaring piece of evidence left carelessly on my doorstep. My breath hitched as I recognized the return address—the previous tenant's. A slow, simmering heat began to spread through my core. I picked it up, my grip white-knuckled, and carried it into my living room. I didn't bother with the box; I just held it, letting the sheer audacity of the situation wash over me. I paced back and forth, my footsteps sharp against the hardwood floor, my gaze fixed on the offending item. "How could they just leave this?" I hissed to the empty room, the sound vibrating with suppressed fury. I wanted to smash it against the wall, to make a scene, but I forced myself to stop, gripping the package so tightly my knuckles were aching.

[story 3]

The doorbell rang, and I opened the door to find the unwelcome parcel waiting there. My eyes immediately scanned the address, and a profound sense of annoyance washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I took the box, my movements stiff and jerky. I didn't offer a greeting; the simple act of accepting the item felt like an insult. I carried it to the kitchen, setting it down with a deliberate, heavy thud. I leaned against the counter, crossing my arms tightly across my chest, my gaze fixed on the contents—some old, dusty tools, judging by the shape. A sharp, involuntary exhale escaped me. "Unbelievable," I breathed out, the word laced with pure exasperation. I wanted to find the sender immediately and demand an explanation, but all I could manage was a rigid set of my shoulders and a slow, deliberate shake of my head.
